coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions and fluorescent ink
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yu-Xuan
Lee, Jan-Yee
Lee, Hsin
Hu, Cho-Chun
Chiu, Tai-Chia
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-step hydrothermal strategy using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and fluorescein as the precursors for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The obtained NCDs exhibited blue fluorescence with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 350/447 nm and fluorescence quantum yield was up to 61%. The blue emission of the NCDs was independent of the excitation wavelength from 330 to 390 nm, while they possessed spherical morphology. The NCDs were also very stable in a wide pH range and in solutions with high ionic strength. More specifically, the fluorescence of the NCDs was effectively quenched by mercuric (Hg2+) and hypochlorite (ClO−) ions, suggesting that the NCDs can be utilized for detecting Hg2+ and ClO− with high selectivity and sensitivity. The linear ranges and limits of detection were 0.05–30 μM and 19 nM for Hg2+, and 0.1–70 μM and 90 nM for ClO−, respectively. Moreover, the NCDs were applied for detecting Hg2+ and ClO− in water samples with satisfactory results, indicating its sensing potential in environmental monitoring. Additionally, it was proven that the synthesized NCDs could be utilized as a fluorescent ink.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent ink
Hydrothermal
Hypochlorite ion
Mercuric ion
Optical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions
Detection of Hg2+ and ClO− in water samples
Synthesis and characterization of the NCDs
Selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ and ClO− sensing
Quenching mechanism for sensing Hg2+ and ClO− ions
Detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images, inset: size distributions of the NCDs; (b) UV–Vis absorption (cyan), fluorescence excitation (green), and emission (blue) spectra of the NCDs, inset: optical images under daylight (left) and a UV lamp (right).
(a) Full scan XPS spectrum and the corresponding high resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s XPS spectra of the NCDs.
Effect of the (a) solution pH, (b) ionic strength, (c) UV exposure time, and (d) storage time on the fluorescence intensities of the NCDs.

(a) Selectivity of the NCDs for Hg2+ (100 μM) over other metal ions (100 μM, blue bars) and their interference (1 mM, green bars); (b) selectivity of the NCDs for ClO− (100 μM) over other anions (1 mM, blue bars) and their interference (100 μM, green bars).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs with the addition of varying Hg2+ concentrations from 0 to 100 μM. The inset shows the linear relationship between the I0/I ratio and the Hg2+ concentration in the 0.05–30 μM range. The regression equation was y = 1.00142 + 0.07502x. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs with the addition of ClO− concentrations from 0 to 100 μM. The inset shows the linear relationship between log(I0/I) and ClO− concentrations ranging between 0.1–70 μM. The regression equation was y = 0.00973 + 0.02039x.
A photographic image of the fluorescent words printed using the developed NCDs as a fluorescent ink under (a, b) day light and (c, d) UV light illumination.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of the NCDs and their applications for sensing Hg2+ and ClO− ions and fluorescent ink.
QY of the NCDs under various synthetic conditions.
Determination of Hg2+ and ClO− ions in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions and fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, Taitung, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, Taitung, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environment Engineering, Kun Shan University, Tainan, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environment Engineering, Kun Shan University, Tainan, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, 369, Section 2, University Road, Taitung 95092, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-step hydrothermal strategy using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and fluorescein as the precursors for the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The obtained NCDs exhibited blue fluorescence with maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 350/447 nm and fluorescence quantum yield was up to 61%. The blue emission of the NCDs was independent of the excitation wavelength from 330 to 390 nm, while they possessed spherical morphology. The NCDs were also very stable in a wide pH range and in solutions with high ionic strength. More specifically, the fluorescence of the NCDs was effectively quenched by mercuric (Hg2+) and hypochlorite (ClO−) ions, suggesting that the NCDs can be utilized for detecting Hg2+ and ClO− with high selectivity and sensitivity. The linear ranges and limits of detection were 0.05–30 μM and 19 nM for Hg2+, and 0.1–70 μM and 90 nM for ClO−, respectively. Moreover, the NCDs were applied for detecting Hg2+ and ClO− in water samples with satisfactory results, indicating its sensing potential in environmental monitoring. Additionally, it was proven that the synthesized NCDs could be utilized as a fluorescent ink.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution from heavy metal ions has become an urgent concern because of their bioaccumulation in the environment and the potential risks to human health [1–3]. Due to its powerful affinity with the thiol groups in intracellular proteins and enzymes, the mercuric ion (Hg2+) is one of the most hazardous species. The excessive accumulation of Hg in the body can cause serious damage to DNA, central nervous system, liver, kidney, and intestinal and even lead to death [4–6]. In addition, the hypochlorite ions (ClO−) have been widely used as powerful oxidants and disinfectants in our daily lives as e.g., water disinfection, sewage purification, and cooling-water treatment agents [7,8]. However, the excessive ClO− concentration may lead to pathological disorders including atherosclerosis, lung and kidney injury, and various cancer types [9]. Therefore, it is important to develop reliable, rapid, sensitive, and selective methods for the determination of low Hg2+ and ClO− concentrations in complex matrices.

To date, several analytical methods have been developed for the determination of Hg2+ and ClO− with high accuracy and sensitivity [10–12]. However, they require sophisticated and expensive instrument and complicated sample preparation procedures, which limit their widespread application. Therefore, the development of various approaches for the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− with high selectivity and sensitivity is still an imperative need. Recently, fluorescence sensing approaches have attracted increasing interest due to the excellent sensitivity, fast response, and simple operation [13,14].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based nanoparticles with a size less than 10 nm. Compared to the conventional fluorescent probes, such as organic dyes, quantum dots, and metal nanoclusters, CDs outperform due to their low cost, simple synthesis, chemical inertness, easy functionalization, excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, great water solubility, and lower toxicity [15,16]. Thus, CDs have been used in many practical applications including chemical sensing, photocatalysts, and bioimaging/biosensing [17–20], while various strategies, such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal, microwave, combustion, and electrochemical approaches, have been adopted for their synthesis [21–25]. Nevertheless, most reported CDs exhibit relatively weak fluorescence intensity and low quantum yield (QY). Therefore, several strategies, such as heteroatom doping, surface functionalization, and passivation of the CDs, have been applied to effectively tune their energy levels, electronic density, and local chemical properties, further improving the CDs-based probe performance [16]. Recently, nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) were synthesized using ethanolamine and 1-carboxyethyl-3-methyl imidazole chloride as the precursor [26], while nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped CDs with a fluorescence QY of 16% were prepared by an one-pot hydrothermal approaches [27].

In this work, a new approach was developed for the facile synthesis of highly fluorescent NCDs by the hydrothermal of citric acid, ethylenediamine, and fluorescein in one step. The obtained NCDs were used to detect Hg2+ and ClO− ions (Scheme 1 ) and as a fluorescent ink. Moreover, the synthesized NCDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence with a high relative QY (61%), good aqueous solubility and photostability, and narrow size distribution. Interestingly, both Hg2+ and ClO− significantly quenched the fluorescence of the NCDs with high efficiency. Furthermore, this sensing system has been successfully applied to the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− in water samples with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, fluorescein, quinine sulfate, sodium hydroxide, trisodium phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, phosphoric acid, anhydrous metal salts LiNO3, NaNO3, KNO3, Mg(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, Ba(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Pb(NO3)2, FeCl3, Co(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, ZnCl2, HgCl2, HAuCl4, NaCl, NaBr, KI, Na2SO4, H2C2O4, KCl, NaNO2, NaBH4, ascorbic acid, Na2SO3, Na3C6H5O7, and NaClO were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The chemicals and reagents used in this study were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

UV–vis spectra were measured using a U-2900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and the fluorescence spectra were obtained using a RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The infrared (IR) spectra were recorded on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA), while the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a JEM-2100 electronic microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan).

The NCDs were prepared using an one-pot hydrothermal treatment. In brief, 420 mg citric acid, 3.0 mg fluorescein, and 536 μL ethylenediamine were added to DI water (10 mL) upon stirring. The mixture was subsequently transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at a constant temperature of 240 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane (Millipore, USA) to remove large particles, dialyzed using DI water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500 Da) for 6 h to remove tiny fragments and any remaining salts, and stored in the dark at 4 °C. The resulting NCDs exhibited blue luminescence under UV light at 365 nm.

The relative QY of the NCDs was calculated according to an established method [28]. The fluorescence intensity of quinine sulfate (Q R = 0.54) dissolved in a 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution was selected as standard, and the QY was calculated as follows:
Q NCDs = Q R × (I NCDs/I R) × (A R/A NCDs) × (n 2 NCDs/n 2 R)
where Q R is the QY of the reference compound, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and n is the reflective index of the solvent (1.33 for water),. To minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorbance was kept under 0.05 at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized NCDs were then used for the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions. For Hg2+ sensing, 200 μL phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 5.0), 600 μL DI water, 1000 μL NCDs solution, and 200 μL different concentrations (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10, 30, 50, 70, 100 μM) of Hg2+ ions were mixed and incubated at room temperature for 30 min. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was measured by recoding the change in the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) in the absence and presence of metal ions at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 and 447 nm, respectively. The same procedure was also followed for the detection of ClO− ions. The selectivity and interference test of the NCDs system were performed under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and ClO− in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of the NCDs in water samples, drinking and tap water samples with spiking 5 μM and 10 μM Hg2+ or ClO−, respectively, were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through 0.22 μm membranes. The applicability of the NCDs system was thus assessed based on the recovery of the Hg2+ and ClO− ions in the utilized water samples. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental parameters, i.e., the fluorescein weight, duration time, and temperature, were investigated to achieve the high fluorescence QY. The effect of the fluorescein weight was first examined by selecting the reaction temperature of 240 °C and a duration of treatment of 4 h. As presented in Table 1 , all the NCDs were formed at various weights of fluorescein, while the highest fluorescence QY of 61% was achieved. Hence, based on their further application in fluorescence sensing, the NCDs discussed in the following sections were synthesized at 240 °C for 4 h using 420 mg citric acid, 536 μL ethylenediamine, and 3.0 mg fluorescein.

The NCDs were prepared through a facile one-step hydrothermal method and characterized by TEM, UV–vis, fluorescence, FT-IR spectroscopy, and XPS. The TEM image (Fig. 1 a) revealed that the NCDs had a spherical-like shape with an average size of 3.6 nm.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometry were used to investigate the optical properties of the NCDs. Two obvious absorption bands centered at 237 and 350 nm were detected in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1b). The absorption band at 237 nm was attributed to the π–π* electronic transitions of the CC bond, which is a typical characteristic of CDs [26,29], whereas the other one at 350 nm was attributed to the n–π* electronic transitions of the CO bond [26,29], revealing the presence of carbonyl or conjugated carbonyl groups. Additionally, the maximum wavelengths of excitation and emission were 350 and 447 nm, respectively. Thus, the NCDs emitted a strong blue luminescence. Moreover, the emission peaks remained almost stable at 447 nm as the excitation wavelengths increased from 330 to 390 nm (Fig. S1). The independence of the fluorescence feature from excitation could be related to a few surface defects and the narrow size distribution of the doped CDs [30]. Moreover, the relative fluorescence QY of the NCDs was calculated to be 61%, which could originate from the emissive traps of the nitrogen-doped surface [16].

In the FT-IR spectrum of the NCDs (Fig. S2), a broad peak detected at 3270 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of the NH and OH bonds [31]. Moreover, the intense peaks at 1651 and 1539 cm−1 were attributed to the CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively [31], while the peak at 1386 cm−1 was due to the CN stretching vibration of the amide [32]. The above results indicated that abundant amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups were present on the surface of the NCDs, which were related to their excellent solubility.

Based on the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 2 a) carbon (C1s, 285 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 400 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 531 eV) elements existed in the NCDs in 66.65%, 15.57%, and 17.78%, respectively, indicating that the nitrogen atoms were successfully doped into the NCDs. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) could be fitted into three distinct peaks at 284.7, 286.1, and 287.7 eV, which corresponded to CC or CC, CN or CO, and CO bonds, respectively [29]. Moreover, the two peaks in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) indicated the existence of two nitrogen species, which in turn confirmed the existence of both CNC (399.7 eV) and NH (401.0 eV) bonds [29]. Furthermore, two peaks at 530.9 and 531.9 eV could be identified in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), which were attributed to the CO and COH bonds, respectively [31]. The FT-IR and XPS analyses also confirmed that the NCDs were doped with nitrogen atoms and functionalized with hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and amide groups, which could disperse well in water without further chemical modifications.

The fluorescence stability of the NCDs is crucial for their practical application. Thus, the effect of the pH, ionic strength, and photo irradiation on the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs was examined (Fig. 3 ). A pH range of 2.0–12.0 was applied, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs was highly stable in a wide pH range between 4.0 and 10.0 (Fig. 3a). However, a decrease in the solution pH from 4.0–2.0 gradually increased the protonation of the NCDs, thus producing higher concentrations of protonated nitrogen atoms on the surface of the NCDs, whereas an increase in the solution pH from 10.0–12.0 favored the deprotonation of the NCDs [33].

Additionally, different concentrations of sodium chloride solution were applied, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs remained almost unchanged even in high ionic strength solutions (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, the NCDs showed excellent photostability, as the fluorescence intensity did not change even after continuous excitation with a UV lamp for 60 min (Fig. 3c) and storage at 4 °C in the dark for about 30 days (Fig. 3d). Collectively, all these features indicated their great potential as highly fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ and ClO− sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the NCDs for the detection of Hg2+ and ClO−, the fluorescence response of the NCDs to other coexisting ions was examined. As presented in Fig. 4 a, most metal ions had negligible or no quenching effect except for Hg2+. In addition, the fluorescence of the NCDs was remarkably quenched upon the addition of 100 μM Hg2+ in the presence of other ions in 10 times concentration (1 mM), suggesting that the interfering ions did not affect the fluorescence intensity. The sensing system was also selective to ClO− detection, as observed in Fig. 4b, while the coexistence of other anionic ions had negligible quenching effects. Consequently, the NCDs were predominantly selective for the Hg2+ and ClO− ions.

To enhance the sensitivity of the developed sensing system, the effect of the reaction time on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was also explored. The fluorescence intensity of the NCDs in the presence of 100 μM Hg2+ and ClO− was investigated at increasing reaction times (0–30 min). After 10 min of reaction, the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs dropped below 50 %, while almost no change was observed in the next 20 min, suggesting that the NCDs interacted rapidly with the Hg2+ and ClO− ions and reached a steady state in 10 min (Fig. S3).

The sensitivity study of the emission spectra of the NCDs provided a useful correlation between the quenched fluorescence intensity and the concentration of the Hg2+ and ClO− ions. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the increase of the Hg2+ concentration from 0 to 100 μM resulted in a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs. Moreover, the I0/I ratio was linearly dependent to the concentration in the 0.05–30 μM range with a correlation efficient (R2) of 0.994 (Fig. 5a inset). LOD was calculated as 19 nM by triplicating the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements and k is the slope of the calibration curve) [26]. Comparing the LOD values with previous reports for Hg2+ sensing (Table S1), the NCDs were proven remarkably sensitive, thus indicating their possible application to Hg2+ ion sensing in water samples.

Similarly, the quenched fluorescence of the NCDs in the presence of ClO− ions was investigated in the 0–100 μM range (Fig. 5b). As seen in the inset of Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity [log (I0/I)] was linearly related to the ClO− concentration in the range of 0.1–70 μM with a good correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.996). The calculated LOD (3σ/k) of ClO− was found to be 90 nM. The obtained results were compared with the reported literatures in Table S2. The proposed method had broad linear range and similar LODs. The sensitivity analysis suggested that the synthesized NCDs can be used as excellent probes for the determination of Hg2+ and ClO− ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism for sensing Hg2+ and ClO− ions
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have been widely used to detect a variety of cations and anions in aqueous solutions, while most of the CDs-based nanosensors were designed as fluorescence "turn-off" probes. Based on the changes in the CDs lifetime, the fluorescence quenching processes can be classified as static or dynamic [34]. Herein, the fluorescence decay curves of the NCDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+/ClO− ions are depicted in Fig. S4. The fluorescence lifetimes were found to be 15.6 ns in the absence of both Hg2+ and ClO−, 12.2 ns in the presence of Hg2+, and 12.3 ns in the presence of ClO−, respectively, using a single exponential model. Thus, an electron transfer took place between the Hg2+/ClO− ions and the NCDs, implying that the fluorescence quenching process was dynamic [35–37]. In addition, the corresponding emission spectra indicated that the fluorescence intensity at 447 nm was decreased by about 83.8% and 96.9% when adding 100 μM Hg2+ and ClO−, respectively. The abundant functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen species on the surface of the prepared NCDs could also coordinate or chelate with Hg2+ to form stable complexes, which led to the transfer of electrons from the NCDs to Hg2+ that ultimately resulted in the fluorescence quenching [38]. In comparison, the fluorescence quenching mechanism for ClO− sensing may be due to the oxidizing ability of the ClO− ions [39]. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra confirmed the presence of several hydroxyl groups on the surface of the NCDs. Thus, a redox reaction between ClO− and the NCDs surface hydroxyl groups could lead to the fluorescence quenching [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and ClO− ions in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed method was evaluated by determining Hg2+ and ClO− in spiked tap and drinking water samples. As shown in Table 2 , the concentrations of the Hg2+ and ClO− ions in the water samples were in good agreement with the spiked concentrations. Namely, the obtained recoveries were in the acceptable ranges of 87%92% and 82%91%, while the RSD was less than 3.2% and 2.1% for the Hg2+ and ClO− ion detection, respectively. The results implied that the proposed method was promising as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the analysis of Hg2+ and ClO− ions.

One of the popular uses of fluorescent ink is in information storage and encryption, while CDs can be used for various applications due to their excellent fluorescent properties [40–42]. Therefore, in this study, the NCDs were diluted with DI water to colorless and then directly injected into a vacant cartridge of a commercial inkjet printer and the highly fluorescent words could be easily printed. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the printed words on the filter paper were colorless under ambient light, but turned into bright blue fluorescent under UV light, thus indicating the applicability of the NCDs as fluorescent ink.

In conclusion, we have prepared highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs via a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and fluorescein as precursors. The obtained NCDs exhibited a uniform spherical morphology, a high fluorescent QY of 61%, excellent fluorescence properties, good water solubility, and high stability. In particular, the NCDs could successfully detect Hg2+ and ClO− ions with high sensitivity and selectivity, while their estimated LODs were 19 and 90 nM, respectively. Moreover, the applicability of the NCDs as a "turn-off" fluorescent probe for the detection of Hg2+ and ClO− in spiked water samples was demonstrated with satisfactory recoveries, which broadens their applications for environmental sensing in the future. In addition, the NCDs could be directly used as a fluorescent ink owing to their excellent fluorescence stability.

Yu-Xuan Li: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Validation. Jan-Yee Lee: Software, Data curation. Hsin Lee: Data curation. Cho-Chun Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Tai-Chia Chiu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under contract number MOST 109-2113-M-143-002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112931.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable technologies for water purification from heavy metals: review and analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Bolisetty, M. Peydayesh, R. Mezzenga, Sustainable technologies for water purification from heavy metals: Review and analysis, Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 463–487. https://doi.org/10.1039/C8CS00493E.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in carbon quantum dot-based sensing of heavy metals in water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Devi, P. Rajput, A. Thakur, K.-H. Kim, P. Kumar, Recent advances in carbon quantum dot-based sensing of heavy metals in water, Trends Anal. Chem. 114 (2019) 171–195. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trac.2019.03.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterial-based optical chemical sensors for the detection of heavy metals in water: recent advances and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Ullah, M. Mansha, I. Khan, A. Qurashi, Nanomaterial-based optical chemical sensors for the detection of heavy metals in water: Recent advances and challenges, Trends Anal. Chem. 100 (2018) 155–166. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trac.2018.01.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mercury in aquatic fauna contamination: a systematic review on its dynamics and potential health risks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. de Almeida Rodrigues, R.G. Ferrari, L.N. dos Santos, C.A. Conte Junior, Mercury in aquatic fauna contamination: A systematic review on its dynamics and potential health risks, J. Environ. Sci. 84 (2019) 205–218. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jes.2019.02.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exposure to mercury and human reproductive health: a systematic review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.C. Henriques, S. Loureiro, M. Fardilha, M.T. Herdeiro, Exposure to mercury and human reproductive health: A systematic review, Reprod. Toxicol. 85 (2019) 93–103. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.reprotox.2019.02.012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The relationship between mercury exposure and epigenetic alterations regarding human health, risk assessment and diagnostic strategies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Khan, S. Momtaz, M. Abdollahi, The relationship between mercury exposure and epigenetic alterations regarding human health, risk assessment and diagnostic strategies, J. Trace Elem. Med. Bio. 52 (2019) 37–47. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtemb.2018.11.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in chromogenic and fluorogenic chemosensors for hypochlorous acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yue, F. Huo, C. Yin, J.O. Escobedo, R.M. Strongin, Recent progress in chromogenic and fluorogenic chemosensors for hypochlorous acid, Analyst 141 (2016) 1859–1873. https://doi.org/10.1039/C6AN00158K.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic regulation of effective detection for hypochlorite based on a dual-mode probe by employing aggregation induced emission (AIE) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Gu, X. Li, Z. Zhou, R. Liao, J. Gao, Y. Tang, Q. Wang, Synergistic regulation of effective detection for hypochlorite based on a dual-mode probe by employing aggregation induced emission (AIE) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effects, Chem. Eng. J. 368 (2019) 157–164. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.02.175.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparative study of Cl,N-Cdots and N-Cdots and application for trinitrophenol and ClO− sensor and cell-imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, L. Zhang, X. Guo, W. Dong, R. Wang, S. Shuang, X. Gong, C. Dong, Comparative study of Cl,N-Cdots and N-Cdots and application for trinitrophenol and ClO− sensor and cell-imaging, Anal. Chim. Acta 1091 (2019) 76–87. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.09.019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical mercury biosensors based on advanced nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Liu, Z. Chu, W. Jin, Electrochemical mercury biosensors based on advanced nanomaterials, J. Mater. Chem. B 7 (2019) 3620–3632. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9TB00418A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection and removal of heavy metal ions: a review
